title: Bank Reconciliation & Cash Posting
subhead: Why the bank balance and the book balance never match on day one, how cash posting actually clears open AR, and the methodical hunt for the real errors hiding inside the timing differences.
hook: "Walk me through a bank reconciliation." After this module your answer is not generic — it names the two-sided method, the $336,250 adjusted balance, and the duplicate deposit you traced and corrected. That is the answer that gets the offer.

chunks.title: Why the bank balance and the book balance almost never match — and shouldn't
chunks.bodyHtml:
At any given moment, your books and the bank's records are usually out of sync — and that is normal. The differences are timing differences, not errors:

  • Deposits in transit — you recorded a customer payment, but it has not hit the bank yet.
  • Outstanding checks — you wrote and recorded a check, but the vendor hasn't cashed it yet.
  • Bank fees and interest credits — the bank moved money you haven't booked yet.

The job of reconciliation is to prove that, once you adjust for timing, the adjusted bank balance and the adjusted book balance are exactly equal. If they are not equal after timing adjustments, there is a real error — a duplicate, a transposition, a missing transaction — and you do not touch the books until you find it.

Skarion January scenario: the bank statement shows $312,450 and the GL shows $301,848. The $10,602 gap is not one thing — it is four timing differences plus one real error. Reconciliation is how you separate the innocent timing from the actual mistake.

chunks.title: The two-sided reconciliation
chunks.bodyHtml:
Every reconciliation works from both directions and they have to meet in the middle:

From the bank sideFrom the book side
Ending balance per bank statementEnding balance per your ledger (GL)
+ Deposits in transit+ Bank credits not yet recorded (interest, etc.)
− Outstanding checks− Bank debits not yet recorded (fees, NSF, etc.)
− Real errors found on the bank side− Real errors found on the book side (duplicate deposits, transpositions)
= Adjusted bank balance= Adjusted book balance

The required result: adjusted bank balance = adjusted book balance — always, exactly, to the cent. If they do not equal after all timing adjustments are applied, there is a mistake still on the table. Find it before you post a single correcting entry.

callout.html: Never force the books to match the bank. Changing a GL number to equal the bank statement is how errors get buried and fraud gets hidden. The whole point is to explain every cent of the difference, not erase it.

chunks.title: Applying client payments to open AR
chunks.bodyHtml:
Cash posting is the act of matching incoming cash receipts to specific open AR invoices and clearing them in the subledger. It is not automatic — clients pay late, partially, combine multiple invoices in one wire, or deduct disputed amounts without telling you.

The cash posting workflow:

  1. Receive bank notification of the incoming wire (e.g. $480,000 from Atlantic Fiber Co.).
  2. Open the AR subledger and identify the open invoice(s) that match the remittance.
  3. Confirm remittance advice from the client — chase it if they didn't send one. Never guess which invoice a payment belongs to.
  4. Post the receipt: Debit Cash / Credit AR — and close the specific invoice in the subledger so it stops aging.
  5. If the amount doesn't exactly match: document the short payment, open a dispute ticket, or credit the difference with manager approval. Never silently write off the gap.

chunks.kicker: Skarion JE builder — wire + bank fee
chunks.title: Post a customer wire with an incoming wire fee
chunks.scenarioHtml: Atlantic Fiber Co. wires $480,000 to settle their open HLD invoice. The bank charges a $25 incoming wire fee, so the net deposit posted to Operating Checking is $479,975. Record the cash in, the fee to expense, and the clearance of the full $480,000 receivable.

chunks.title: Apply a partial / disputed payment
chunks.scenarioHtml: Southeast Broadband wires only $190,000 of their $405,000 balance with a note that the remainder is "in dispute." Apply the cash to their oldest invoice and clear that much of the receivable. The disputed remainder stays open in the AR subledger with a dispute flag — it is not written off.

chunks.title: A customer check bounces — reinstate the receivable
chunks.scenarioHtml: TeleCom Builders sends a $50,000 check, which is recorded in the books. The check bounces. The bank reverses the $50,000 deposit and charges a $35 NSF fee. Reinstate the receivable (the customer owes us again) and record the fee the bank charged us.

chunks.title: The six most common reconciliation problems

cards.back: The same customer payment is entered in the GL twice. Fix: Debit Accounts Receivable / Credit Cash to reverse the duplicate and reinstate the receivable. Skarion found DEP-0091 ($10,547.10) duplicated.
A check written and recorded in the books that the vendor hasn't cashed yet. Subtract it from the bank side — it simply hasn't cleared. Skarion: check #2847 to Global CAD for $4,200.
The book entry doesn't match what cleared. Rent recorded as $12,750 but the check cleared at $12,800 — a $50 correcting entry is required on the book side.
A monthly service charge, wire fee, or NSF fee hit the bank but was never entered in the books. Book it on the book side: Debit Bank Fees / Credit Cash.
A last-day deposit recorded in the books that has not posted to the bank statement yet. Add it to the bank side — it will appear next month.
An unauthorized ACH debit. Flag immediately, file a bank dispute within the allowed window, and post Debit Due from Bank (or AR) / Credit Cash while the investigation runs.

chunks.title: Skarion January reconciliation — finding the real error
chunks.bodyHtml:
Here is the actual January reconciliation, worked both directions. This is the example you'll reference in interviews.

Bank side:

Bank sideAmount
Bank statement ending balance$312,450.00
+ Deposits in transit (Dec 31 receipt)+$28,000.00
− Outstanding check #2847 (Global CAD)−$4,200.00
= Adjusted bank balance$336,250.00

Book side:

Book sideAmount
GL balance$346,823.60
− Unrecorded bank fee−$45.00
+ Unrecorded interest+$18.50
− Duplicate deposit (DEP-0091, Southeast Broadband)−$10,547.10
= Adjusted book balance$336,250.00 ✓

Both sides meet at $336,250.00 — exactly. The duplicate deposit was the real error hiding inside the timing differences. Now post three correcting entries on the book side.

chunks.title: Correct the DEP-0091 duplicate deposit
chunks.scenarioHtml: Customer deposit DEP-0091 ($10,547.10 from Southeast Broadband) was entered twice in the GL. Reverse the duplicate and reinstate the receivable — revenue was never overstated, only cash and AR were.

chunks.title: Book the unrecorded monthly bank fee
chunks.scenarioHtml: The bank charged a $45.00 monthly service fee that was never entered in the books. Record the expense and the corresponding reduction in cash.

chunks.title: Book the unrecorded interest credit
chunks.scenarioHtml: The bank credited $18.50 of interest income on the operating account that was never recorded in the books. Cash increases and Interest Income is recognized.

chunks.intro: Four bank-rec questions, the kind that come up in interviews and in the real first month of the job.
chunks.title: Bank reconciliation quiz

questions.q: A client wires $240,000. Your bank charges a $35 incoming wire fee. What is the net debit to Cash (the amount that actually lands in the operating account)?
questions.explanation: Net cash landing is $240,000 − $35 = $239,965. The full entry: Debit Cash $239,965, Debit Bank Fees $35, Credit AR $240,000. The gross receivable is cleared; the fee is booked to its own expense line.

questions.q: A check written to an offshore contractor on January 28th has not cleared by January 31st. Which side of the reconciliation is it on?
options: Add it to the book side
Subtract it from the bank side as an outstanding check
Add it to the bank side as a deposit in transit
Ignore it — it cleared the books already
questions.explanation: It is in your books but not yet on the bank statement — a classic outstanding check. Subtract it from the bank statement balance when computing the adjusted bank balance.

questions.q: The adjusted bank balance is $336,250 and the adjusted book balance is $336,290. What is true?
options: There is still a $40 unexplained error to find before touching the books
Post a $40 plug entry to make them equal
questions.explanation: After all timing adjustments they must be exactly equal. A $40 remaining gap means a real error is still undiscovered — keep tracing source transactions until the gap is fully explained.

questions.q: Skarion finds a $10,547.10 duplicate deposit in the GL. What is the correcting entry?
questions.explanation: The duplicate over-credited Cash and over-credited AR. Reverse it: Debit AR (reinstate the receivable), Credit Cash (remove the duplicate). Revenue was never affected.

chunks.title: Excel bank reconciliation lab — three months
chunks.instructionsHtml:
Download the bank statement workbooks below and the Excel reconciliation template (Template 01). Each statement has a Bank Statement tab, a GL Cash register, and a Reconciliation Worksheet. For each month:

  1. Match every transaction between the bank statement and the register.
  2. Identify the seeded problems (duplicates, wrong amounts, outstanding checks, unrecorded fees — January has 4 items to find and 2 correcting entries).
  3. Complete the two-sided worksheet until the adjusted balances are exactly $336,250.00 for January.

Watch the labels — the March worksheet has one duplicate that is a duplicated debit, unlike January's duplicated credit.
chunks.reflectionPrompt: Write your January reconciliation memo: what were the four items, how did you find the duplicate, and what were your two correcting entries?
